Q1: I tried to set aside time on weekday evenings after work and activities, usually around 7 or 8 PM. Being a visual learner, I'd spread out my notes and diagrams on the kitchen table and work through things that way. But honestly, my routine was inconsistent—some weeks I'd manage 18 hours of solid study, other weeks life just got in the way and I'd barely touch anything. I always intended to be more organized, but the sadness I've been feeling lately made it hard to stick to plans.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was due and how I was feeling emotionally. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks my motivation would just disappear. I tried to study on Tuesday and Thursday evenings consistently, but that fell apart midway through the semester. The stress of balancing everything made it hard to maintain any real rhythm.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there were new announcements or if an assignment deadline was coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to see the video lectures because I knew I needed to catch up. If I'm being honest, I didn't feel drawn to it—it was more obligation than habit. The platform never felt particularly inviting to me.
Q4: Not really, no. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, maybe for a week or two, but it never became automatic for me. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped my mood somewhat, but they didn't translate into better LMS habits. I think because I don't really use educational technology much in general, it always felt like an extra step rather than something natural.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful because I could pause them and sketch out diagrams and visual notes. I watched almost 300 minutes of video content because seeing concepts explained visually really helps things click for me. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained things in different ways. But overall, I wished there were more visual resources like infographics or flowcharts.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials that were just walls of text—they didn't work for my learning style at all. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond the minimum because I felt too drained to put myself out there socially. Some of the supplementary resources seemed useful but I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, and later never came. My low energy made it easy to skip anything that wasn't absolutely required.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, or if it presented information visually. If something helped me create mental pictures or diagrams, I'd invest in it. But if it felt like busywork or if the connection to my grade wasn't clear, my motivation—which is already pretty low—just wouldn't carry me through. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent wisely.
Q8: Optional activities felt almost impossible to prioritize given everything else going on. Required ones at least had the pressure of consequences, which sometimes pushed me past my low motivation. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then the sadness would settle in and I'd convince myself it wasn't essential. There was guilt about skipping them, but not enough to actually change my behavior.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and video transcripts, then print them out so I could highlight and annotate by hand. I'm very visual, so I'd create my own diagrams and mind maps in a physical notebook that obviously wouldn't show up anywhere online. I also used a textbook from the library and some YouTube videos that explained concepts in more visual ways. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from the lectures, color-coding my notes, and creating visual summaries. None of that shows up in any log. I'd also study with a friend from another section sometimes, working through problems together at a café. My LMS activity might show I watched a video once, but it wouldn't show that I paused it fifty times to sketch things out.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download the slides or save video links, watch them once online, and then work from my printed or downloaded versions for days afterward. My study style involves a lot of repetition and visual reorganization that doesn't require being logged in. The initial access was just the starting point—the real studying happened offline with my colored pens and notebooks.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was dealing with a lot of sadness—just this persistent low feeling that made getting out of bed difficult, let alone engaging with coursework. My extracurricular commitments didn't let up, and I felt stretched thin. The stress built up even though my anxiety stayed relatively low; it was more like a heavy weight than panic. That's when my assignment completion really dropped.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect because it felt like extra effort compared to just studying from my notes. Competing responsibilities from my activities and life stuff meant I'd often choose the path of least resistance. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than seeking help. The medium stress level I carried made everything feel harder than it probably was.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about everything weighing on me. Sometimes I'd open discussion posts and read them but feel too disconnected to actually process what people were saying. My body was going through the motions but my brain wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed a lot of assignments even when I knew they mattered. The combination of low motivation and persistent sadness made it easy to tell myself I'd do it tomorrow. Some tasks felt overwhelming before I even started, so avoidance became a coping mechanism. I knew it was hurting my grade—my assignment completion was barely over half—but knowing and doing are different things when you're struggling emotionally.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. Without that external pressure, my motivation wasn't strong enough on its own. I'd often work right up until the deadline, which meant rushed work that didn't reflect what I actually understood. The LMS would probably show bursts of activity right before due dates, which is pretty accurate to how I operated.
Q17: Feedback had mixed effects. When it was encouraging, it gave me a small boost, but that would fade quickly. Critical feedback sometimes made me feel worse and less likely to engage because it confirmed my fears that I wasn't doing well enough. I think I needed more supportive, constructive feedback to help with my low resilience. The mindfulness intervention helped me process negative feedback better toward the end.
Q18: The course structure felt fairly rigid, which was both good and bad. Good because it gave me a framework when I had no internal motivation. Bad because when I fell behind, there wasn't much flexibility to catch up at my own pace. The weekly modules moved on whether I was ready or not, and my attendance was decent but my actual understanding lagged behind. I needed more visual scaffolding built into the structure.
Q19: The heavy reliance on text-based readings made engagement harder for me as a visual learner. When there were diagrams or video explanations, I engaged much more easily. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic, which made them feel like a chore. Having more visual content options and maybe flexible deadlines for people struggling would have helped me stay more connected to the course.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying but struggling—logging in repeatedly, watching videos, reading materials, but then being too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete assignments. It might also be someone dealing with emotional difficulties who's going through the motions without being able to produce finished work. That partially describes me, actually. The intention is there but the execution falls apart.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning can happen offline. Someone might download everything, study intensively from printed materials or external resources, and only log in briefly. I did reasonably well on some assignment scores despite low engagement because my offline studying with visual methods was effective. The LMS activity doesn't capture studying from a textbook or creating hand-drawn diagrams.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logged in, what they clicked on, and submission timestamps. But they miss the quality of attention, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone is actually learning versus just clicking through. They can't see that I spent three hours redrawing a concept until I understood it. They also can't capture that sometimes I was logged in but mentally checked out due to sadness.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning process. It's not just showing up or clicking buttons—it's being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement looks like creating visual notes, asking myself questions, and wanting to understand rather than just pass. This semester, I struggled to feel that genuine connection most of the time, even when I was technically participating.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my emotional state—the persistent sadness that made everything harder, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped my mood even if it didn't fix my grades. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline visual studying or the support I got from friends outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that I actually understood more than my exam score suggests; test-taking under stress doesn't reflect my real learning. My journey this semester was about more than what any log could measure.
